E00.description: Inspector Rothwell crouched beside Edmund Hartley's body in the dimly lit study, his eyes scanning the Persian rug beneath the victim's outstretched hand. A glint of gold caught his attention—a delicate ladies' ring with an ornate flower design, lying just inches from Hartley's fingertips as if it had been dropped or torn away during a struggle. He carefully lifted it with his handkerchief, noting its unusual weight and the peculiar seam running along the inner band, though its significance escaped him for the moment.
E00.narrative:
Inspector Rothwell turned the ring slowly beneath the beam of his penlight, examining the ornate flower petals etched into the gold. "Martinez, look at this engraving on the inside," he said, his voice barely above a murmur. His partner leaned in, squinting at the delicate script that read *"To my dearest G, eternally yours."* The initial struck them both simultaneously—Geoffrey Hartley, Edmund's younger brother, who stood to inherit the entire Hartley estate now that Edmund was dead. Martinez photographed the ring from multiple angles while Rothwell's mind churned through possibilities. Geoffrey had been at the gallery's opening ceremony, had access to Edmund throughout the evening, and according to preliminary statements, the brothers had been locked in a bitter dispute over their father's will for months.

"Bag this as priority evidence," Rothwell instructed, his jaw tightening as he surveyed the study once more. The champagne flute on Edmund's desk still contained a small amount of liquid, its rim smudged with what appeared to be lipstick—an odd detail for a man drinking alone in his study after the gallery event. But it was the ring that troubled him most. Why would Geoffrey's ring—if it was indeed his—be clutched near Edmund's hand? Had Edmund torn it from his brother during a final confrontation? The seam along the band suggested it might open, perhaps a locket-style piece, but Rothwell's attempts to pry it revealed nothing. He made a mental note to have the lab examine both the ring's construction and the champagne's contents.

"Get Geoffrey Hartley's whereabouts for every minute of tonight," Rothwell said, standing and brushing dust from his knees. "And find out if anyone saw him wearing jewelry—specifically a gold ring. If this belongs to

E01.description: Inspector Rothwell and Detective Martinez drove to the Worthington estate to verify Lady Beatrice's claim that she was attending a private dinner party on the night of Edmund Hartley's murder. They interviewed the hostess, Mrs. Eleanor Pembroke, who confirmed Lady Beatrice arrived at eight o'clock but seemed distracted and disappeared for nearly forty minutes between courses, claiming she needed fresh air in the garden. Rothwell made note of the timeline—the estimated time of Hartley's death fell precisely within that unexplained absence.
E01.narrative:
# Alibi Verification

Inspector Rothwell stood in the marble-floored vestibule of Dr. Marcus Pemberton's Harley Street medical practice, studying the physician's immaculate office with its leather-bound volumes and gleaming brass instruments. Detective Martinez had her notebook ready as Dr. Pemberton entered, his silver hair perfectly combed, his manner professionally solicitous. "Terrible business about Edmund," the doctor said, settling behind his mahogany desk. "I've known the Hartley family for decades. How can I help?" Rothwell leaned forward. "We're verifying whereabouts on the night of the murder. Geoffrey Hartley claims he was here at your practice from seven-thirty until nearly nine o'clock, discussing his father's medical history for estate purposes. Can you confirm?" Dr. Pemberton's face showed the slightest hesitation—barely perceptible, but enough to sharpen Rothwell's attention. "Yes, Geoffrey was here," he said slowly, his fingers drumming once against the desk blotter. "Though I must say, Inspector, his behavior struck me as rather... peculiar. He seemed agitated, kept checking his watch, and asked several pointed questions about arsenic poisoning and its symptoms. At the time, I thought perhaps he was researching something for a novel—he'd mentioned writing—but now, given what's happened..."

Martinez glanced up from her notes, catching the implication. "What time exactly did he leave, Doctor?" Pemberton consulted an appointment book with theatrical precision, though Rothwell noticed the page was suspiciously pristine, as if rarely referenced. "Eight forty-five, I believe. Perhaps eight-fifty. He left rather abruptly after I explained how certain poisons could be administered in liquid form without detection. Seemed quite interested in that particular

E02.description: Inspector Rothwell takes the ring to a jewelry expert and forensic specialist to examine the hollow compartment for any residue or concealed materials that might explain its presence at the murder scene. He requests the lab to analyze any trace substances found inside and to determine if the compartment recently held poison, powder, or a small object. Meanwhile, Detective Martinez cross-references the ring's distinctive ornate flower design with local jewelers' records to identify potential owners connected to Edmund Hartley.
E02.narrative:
# The Hollow Chamber

Inspector Rothwell placed the delicate gold ring under the jeweler's loupe, watching as Mr. Castellano, a third-generation craftsman with thirty years of forensic consultation experience, examined the ornate flower design with practiced precision. "Remarkable workmanship," Castellano murmured, his weathered fingers rotating the band beneath the magnification. "Victorian era, I'd say, but the hollow compartment—that's been added much more recently. See here, Inspector? The soldering technique is modern, perhaps within the last five years. This wasn't a jeweler's work, though. Too crude. Someone modified this piece themselves, or had it done quietly." He carefully opened the tiny hinged section concealed within the band's thickness, revealing a chamber no larger than a pea. "Whatever was inside has left a residue—I can see crystalline traces along the interior walls."

The forensic report arrived within forty-eight hours, and Rothwell studied it with mounting unease in the incident room, Martinez reading over his shoulder. The traces were indeed arsenic trioxide—the same compound that had killed Edmund Hartley—in quantities consistent with a single lethal dose. But it was Martinez's follow-up investigation that sent the case spiraling in an unexpected direction. "James, you need to see this," she said, spreading photographs across the desk. "Three jewelers in Mayfair recognized the design immediately. It's from the Hartley family collection—an heirloom piece. Edmund's wife reported it missing from their estate sale inventory six months ago." She tapped a faded catalog entry. "And here's the interesting part: Geoffrey Hartley was the only family member with access to the storage vault during the inventory period. He claimed several items for 'sentimental value,' but this ring wasn't among the

E03.description: Inspector Rothwell returns to Edmund Hartley's study to examine his personal correspondence and desk drawers, searching for any letters or documents that might connect him to Lady Beatrice or explain why her ring was at the scene. He discovers a locked drawer in Hartley's mahogany desk and carefully pries it open, revealing a bundle of letters tied with black ribbon—all written in an elegant feminine hand and signed only with the initial "B." The most recent letter, dated three days before the murder, contains a desperate plea: "Edmund, you promised to keep our secret, but I cannot allow you to destroy everything I've built."
E03.narrative:
Inspector Rothwell had barely finished reading the damning letter when the study door creaked open behind him. Dr. Marcus Pemberton stepped inside, his silver hair catching the afternoon light streaming through the window. "Inspector, I thought I might find you here," he said with practiced concern, his eyes briefly flickering to the bundle of letters on the desk. "I've been wrestling with my conscience all morning about something I witnessed the night of the gala."

The doctor moved closer, his hands clasped behind his back in a gesture of scholarly contemplation. "You see, I happened to pass by the service corridor near the furnace room—I was looking for the gentlemen's facilities, you understand—and I saw Vivienne Ashford there, quite distressed. She was clutching something to her chest, weeping actually. When she noticed me, she composed herself immediately and said she'd just learned that Edmund had been having an affair with Lady Beatrice." Pemberton paused, allowing the revelation to settle. "But here's the thing, Inspector—she told me that Geoffrey had discovered the affair weeks ago. That Geoffrey had been making terrible threats about what he'd do if Edmund humiliated the family name. I didn't think much of it at the time, assumed it was just family drama, but now..." He gestured meaningfully at the letters. "Well, those certainly seem to corroborate an affair, don't they? And Geoffrey's behavior that evening was rather volatile. I saw him arguing with Edmund near the champagne table, actually. His face was absolutely scarlet with rage."

Rothwell studied the doctor's face, noting the helpful earnestness that seemed almost too perfectly timed. Yet the letters in his hand were undeniably real, and Geoffrey Hartley's animosity toward his brother had been documented by multiple witnesses. "Did you hear what they were arguing about

E04.description: Inspector Rothwell and Detective Martinez visit the gentlemen's club where Gregory Sutherland claimed he spent the entire evening of Edmund Hartley's murder playing cards with three associates. Upon interviewing the club steward and checking the guest register, they discover Sutherland did arrive at seven o'clock as stated, but the card game concluded at half-past nine—leaving him unaccounted for during the critical hour between ten and eleven when Hartley was killed. Rothwell requests the names and addresses of Sutherland's card partners to verify the exact timeline of his departure.
E04.narrative:
Inspector Rothwell adjusted his spectacles as he studied the leather-bound guest register, its pages yellowed and smelling faintly of cigar smoke. The gentlemen's club steward, a rail-thin man named Pemberton who wore his livery with military precision, hovered nearby with practiced discretion. "Mr. Sutherland did indeed arrive at seven o'clock, Inspector. I remember quite distinctly—he seemed rather agitated that evening, kept checking his pocket watch."

"And the card game?" Detective Martinez pressed, her pen poised over her notebook.

"Concluded at half-past nine, madam," the steward confirmed. "I served brandy to the gentlemen as they settled their accounts. Mr. Sutherland left shortly thereafter." Rothwell's pulse quickened—the critical hour between ten and eleven, when Edmund Hartley had raised that fatal glass of champagne, remained unaccounted for. But before he could pursue this thread, Dr. Marcus Pemberton emerged from the smoking room, his distinguished gray beard and physician's bearing lending him immediate authority. "Inspector Rothwell? I couldn't help but overhear. I was one of Sutherland's card partners that evening. Poor fellow was quite distraught—kept muttering about Geoffrey Hartley's mismanagement of the family business. Said Geoffrey had been threatening his brother Edmund for weeks over some financial matter. Sutherland left our table early because he couldn't concentrate, said he feared what Geoffrey might do." The doctor's expression was grave, sympathetic. "We all urged him to stay, but he insisted on going home. I suppose guilt weighs heavily when one suspects a family member of murderous intent."

Rothwell exchanged a glance with Martinez, watching as she underlined Geoffrey's name twice in her notes. Another thread connecting the nephew to

E05.description: Inspector Rothwell and Detective Martinez arrange a formal interview with Lady Beatrice at her residence, confronting her directly about the letters signed "B" and the ornate ring found at the murder scene. They present the most recent letter containing the threat about "destroying everything" and ask her to explain her relationship with Edmund Hartley and what secret she feared he would reveal. Rothwell observes her reaction carefully, noting any signs of distress or evasion when pressed about her whereabouts on the night of the murder.
E05.narrative:
Inspector Rothwell had barely settled into the drawing room of Lady Beatrice's Kensington residence when the door opened unexpectedly. Dr. Marcus Pemberton entered with the easy familiarity of an old friend, carrying his medical bag and wearing an expression of practiced concern. "Inspector, Detective Martinez—I hope you don't mind my presence. Lady Beatrice telephoned me earlier, quite distressed about your impending visit. Given her fragile constitution, I thought it prudent to be here." He set his bag down with deliberate care, positioning himself between the detectives and the pale woman seated by the window. When Rothwell produced the letter signed "B" and began his questions about the ornate ring, Pemberton leaned forward with sudden interest. "Good heavens, that ring—I've seen one remarkably similar. Geoffrey Hartley wore his mother's old ring on a chain around his neck. He showed it to me once at the club, said it was his talisman for luck." The doctor's eyes narrowed thoughtfully. "You know, Geoffrey had access to the entire gallery before the opening. He was there for hours, supposedly arranging the champagne service himself."

Lady Beatrice's hands trembled as she reached for her teacup, and Pemberton was immediately at her side. "As for these letters, Inspector, I can explain what Edmund discovered. It wasn't Lady Beatrice's secret at all—it was Geoffrey's embezzlement scheme." He withdrew a folded document from his jacket pocket, smoothing it across the table. "Edmund confided in me weeks ago. He'd found discrepancies in the gallery's accounts, all pointing to his brother. The 'destruction' mentioned in that letter? Geoffrey stood to lose everything when Edmund planned to expose him at the gala." Rothwell studied the financial statement, noting

E06.description: Inspector Rothwell and Detective Martinez question Vivienne Hartley in the drawing room about her private conversation with Edmund on the night of his death, pressing her for specific details about what was discussed and why she appeared distressed afterward according to witness statements. They show her the letters signed "B" without revealing Lady Beatrice's identity yet, asking if she recognized the handwriting or knew of any secrets her husband was keeping. Rothwell watches closely for any indication that Vivienne knew about Edmund's relationship with Lady Beatrice or had discovered something that evening that might have driven her to violence.
E06.narrative:
Inspector Rothwell leaned forward in his chair, his gaze fixed on Vivienne Ashford as she sat composed in the drawing room, her black mourning dress a stark contrast to the pale afternoon light filtering through the windows. "Mrs. Ashford, multiple witnesses place you in an intense private conversation with Edmund thirty minutes before the opening ceremony. What exactly were you discussing?" Martinez stood by the mantelpiece, notebook in hand, watching her carefully. Vivienne's fingers trembled slightly as she reached for her teacup. "Edmund was concerned about some irregularities in the gallery's finances. He'd discovered—" She paused, her voice catching. "He'd found discrepancies he wanted to discuss before making them public."

Before Rothwell could press further, the drawing room door opened and Dr. Marcus Pemberton entered with an apologetic expression. "Forgive the intrusion, Inspector, but I couldn't help overhearing from the hallway. I believe I can clarify matters considerably." He moved to stand beside Vivienne's chair, his presence somehow both protective and authoritative. "You see, I was present for part of that conversation. Edmund had actually summoned Vivienne to inform her of something quite different—he'd discovered that his brother Geoffrey had been systematically embezzling from the gallery's accounts for months. The financial irregularities Vivienne mentions weren't her doing at all." He withdrew a leather-bound ledger from inside his coat. "I took the liberty of retrieving this from Edmund's private office. You'll find Geoffrey's signature on numerous unauthorized withdrawals."

Rothwell accepted the ledger, flipping through pages marked with red ink annotations in Edmund's distinctive hand. The entries were damning—thousands of pounds withdrawn under questionable pretenses, all bearing Geoffrey's authorization. "Why didn't you come forward with this immediately

E07.description: Inspector Rothwell and Detective Martinez travel to the Grand Imperial Hotel where Isabella Rossi claimed she was dining with her business associate, Mr. Chen, between eight and eleven o'clock on the night of Edmund Hartley's murder. They request the reservation records from the maître d' and interview the wait staff who served that evening, asking them to confirm whether Miss Rossi remained at her table throughout the entire dinner service or if she left the premises at any point. Rothwell also asks to see the hotel's guest register to verify if Mr. Chen was indeed staying there as Rossi had stated.
E07.narrative:
The Grand Imperial Hotel's crystal chandeliers cast a warm glow over the burgundy carpeting as Inspector Rothwell and Detective Martinez approached the maître d's podium. The slender man in an immaculate tuxedo produced the reservation ledger with practiced efficiency, his manicured finger tracing down to the entry for that fateful evening. "Ah yes, Miss Rossi's party of two, eight o'clock reservation under the name Chen," he confirmed, though his brow furrowed slightly. Before Rothwell could press further, a waiter emerged from the kitchen—a distinguished gentleman with silver temples whom the maître d' introduced as Pierre, their most senior server. "I remember Miss Rossi quite well that evening," Pierre interjected smoothly, his accent carrying a hint of French refinement. "She and Mr. Chen dined for nearly three hours. I served them myself—the lady had the Dover sole, and her companion ordered our signature Wellington. They never left the table until well after ten-thirty." His certainty seemed absolute, yet Martinez noticed how Pierre's eyes flickered toward the maître d' before continuing. "In fact, I recall commenting to my colleague how devoted she seemed to her business discussion. Never once did she excuse herself, not even to powder her nose."

What neither detective realized was that Pierre—whose real name was Thomas Blackwood—had abandoned his usual post at the gallery's catering company that night to plant himself at the Grand Imperial, anticipating precisely this inquiry. As Rothwell examined the guest register, finding Mr. Chen's signature exactly where it should be, Blackwood watched the inspector's shoulders relax with satisfaction. "Well, that certainly rules out Miss Rossi," Rothwell muttered to Martinez, snapping his notebook closed. "Which brings us back to Geoffrey Hartley. No alibi,

E08.description: Inspector Rothwell and Detective Martinez are reviewing the security footage from the museum's hallway cameras when they notice several staff members and caterers lingering near a partially open office door approximately thirty minutes before the gala's opening ceremony. The timestamps show at least four different individuals pausing in that same spot over a ten-minute window, some glancing toward the door, others seeming to slow their pace as they passed by. Rothwell freezes the frame on the nameplate visible beside the door: "Vivienne Castellane, Curator."
E08.narrative:
Inspector Rothwell leaned closer to the monitor, his finger hovering over the pause button as he studied the faces of those who had lingered outside Vivienne Ashford's office door. "Martinez, pull up the guest list and cross-reference it with the caterer's employee roster. I want to know who each of these people are." As his partner began typing, Rothwell's attention shifted to a detail he'd nearly missed—a figure emerging from the office itself, briefly visible in the doorway's sliver of light. The timestamp read 6:47 PM, precisely thirty-one minutes before Edmund Hartley had raised his champagne glass for the fatal toast. "There," Martinez said, tapping her screen. "Geoffrey Hartley was scheduled to meet with his brother at seven o'clock that evening, according to Edmund's calendar. The meeting location is listed as 'V.A. office.'" She paused, her brow furrowing. "But if witnesses saw Vivienne and Edmund arguing in her office at 6:50, and Geoffrey was supposed to be there at seven..."

Rothwell rewound the footage and played it again, this time focusing on the body language of the figure exiting the office. The person moved with purpose, shoulders squared, hands shoved deep into pockets. "Freeze it there." He pointed at the screen where the figure's profile caught the hallway light for just a moment. The build, the gait—it could easily be Geoffrey Hartley. "The timing puts him at the scene just before the argument between Vivienne and Edmund. What if Geoffrey went to confront his brother first?" Martinez pulled out the evidence log and ran her finger down the entries until she found what she was looking for. "The champagne glasses were pre-poured by the catering staff and arranged on the presentation table at 6

E09.description: Inspector Rothwell and Detective Martinez obtain the building's security footage to review the body language and duration of the confrontation in Vivienne's office, looking for signs of physical aggression or threats. They also interview Vivienne's assistant and nearby office workers to determine what sounds or specific words might have been overheard during the heated exchange. Additionally, they cross-reference phone records to see if either party made any calls immediately before or after this meeting.
E09.narrative:
# Chapter 7: Shadows in the Frame

Inspector Rothwell leaned forward in the gallery's cramped security office, his eyes fixed on the grainy footage as Vivienne Ashford and Edmund Hartley appeared on screen. The timestamp read 6:47 PM—thirty minutes before the fatal toast. Detective Martinez adjusted the playback speed, and they watched in silence as Edmund's gestures grew increasingly animated, his finger jabbing toward Vivienne's desk. Her posture remained rigid, arms crossed, but there was something peculiar about the way she kept her right hand positioned—almost protective, fingers curled inward. "Rewind that last bit," Rothwell muttered. "Look at her hand. She's wearing something that catches the light." Martinez paused the frame, but the resolution was too poor to discern details. What struck Rothwell more was the final moment: Edmund's face twisted with what appeared to be triumph rather than anger as he turned to leave, while Vivienne remained motionless, staring at the closed door for a full forty-three seconds afterward.

"The assistant says she heard shouting," Detective Martinez reported, flipping through her notes after interviewing the gallery staff. "Specifically, she heard Edmund say something about 'Geoffrey's debts' and 'blood money.' One of the junior curators working late in the adjacent office claims he heard Edmund threaten to 'expose everything before the night is over.'" Rothwell's jaw tightened. The phone records had revealed something even more damning: Geoffrey Hartley had called his brother's mobile at 6:52 PM—just minutes after Edmund left Vivienne's office. The call lasted eighteen seconds. At 7:03 PM, Geoffrey had received an incoming call from an unlisted number that pinged off a cell tower near the gallery. "

E10.description: Inspector Rothwell and Detective Martinez drive to the Riverside Golf Club where Arthur Fenwick claimed he was playing a round of golf on the afternoon of Edmund Hartley's murder. They meet with the club's manager to review tee time records and credit card receipts, then locate and interview Fenwick's alleged playing partners—Gerald Moorhouse and Thomas Pritchard—to confirm whether Fenwick remained with them for the entire eighteen holes or left the course at any point between two and six o'clock.
E10.narrative:
# Chapter: The Riverside Alibi

Inspector Rothwell pushed through the heavy oak doors of the Riverside Golf Club, Detective Martinez at his side, their shoes clicking against Italian marble that seemed more suited to a palace than a sporting establishment. The club manager, a meticulously groomed man named Dennison, led them to a private office where leather-bound ledgers sat beside a modern computer terminal. "Arthur Fenwick, you said? Yes, he was here that afternoon," Dennison confirmed, pulling up the digital records. "Tee time at 2:47 PM, paid for drinks afterward at 6:23 PM. One of our regulars." The credit card receipt showed Fenwick's signature, bold and unhesitant—the mark of a man with nothing to hide.

They found Gerald Moorhouse in the club's bar, nursing a scotch beneath a mounted stag's head. The silver-haired gentleman nodded enthusiastically when asked about that particular Tuesday. "Arthur? Of course, we played the full eighteen. Never left our sight—well, except for the usual trips to the bushes, if you catch my meaning." He chuckled, but Rothwell noted how Moorhouse's fingers drummed against his glass. When they pressed about timing, Moorhouse pulled out his own scorecard, still tucked in his golf bag. "See here? We finished at the sixteenth hole around 5:45. Arthur made a remarkable birdie. I remember because I owed him fifty pounds on that hole alone." Thomas Pritchard, their third partner, corroborated every detail when they reached him on the practice green—perhaps too perfectly, Rothwell thought, as if the story had been rehearsed.

"So Fenwick's in the clear," Martinez said as they walked back to their vehicle, though

E11.description: Inspector Rothwell personally delivers the champagne glass fragments to the independent forensic laboratory at Scotland Yard, bypassing the local facility where Dr. Pemberton has influence, and requests a priority analysis for fingerprints, DNA, and any trace substances including toxins or sedatives. Simultaneously, Detective Martinez confronts Dr. Pemberton at his office with the discrepancies in Edmund Hartley's preliminary cause of death, presenting evidence of the suspicious circumstances and formally demanding a complete autopsy be performed by the Home Office pathologist.
E11.narrative:
Inspector Rothwell had barely crossed the threshold of the laboratory when his phone buzzed with an urgent message from Martinez. The confrontation with Dr. Pemberton had taken an unexpected turn. When Rothwell arrived at the medical examiner's office twenty minutes later, he found his partner in heated discussion with a third party—Thomas Blackwood, the gallery's insurance assessor, who had apparently arrived moments after Martinez began questioning Pemberton.

"Inspector Rothwell, thank goodness you're here," Blackwood said smoothly, his silver cufflinks catching the fluorescent light as he gestured with practiced concern. "I've been explaining to Detective Martinez that I have information that might be relevant to your investigation. You see, I was reviewing the gallery's security protocols after Edmund's tragic death, and I discovered something rather disturbing." He produced a leather portfolio, extracting several printed emails with a theatrical flourish. "Geoffrey Hartley had been pressuring Edmund for months about changing the beneficiary on a substantial life insurance policy. These emails show Geoffrey became quite... aggressive when Edmund refused. And here's the troubling part—I witnessed Geoffrey in the storage area near the champagne service station just before the toast. At the time, I thought nothing of it, but given Dr. Pemberton's findings about the suspicious nature of Edmund's collapse..." He trailed off meaningfully, letting the implication hang in the air.

Rothwell studied the emails, his jaw tightening as he noted the dates and increasingly desperate tone. Martinez shot him a glance that said they'd discuss this privately, but Blackwood pressed on, his voice dropping to a confidential murmur. "I understand you've been looking into Mrs. Ashford's conversation with Edmund that evening. I was there, Inspector—I can assure you it was merely about a disputed Modigliani authentication. Heated

E12.description: Inspector Rothwell crouched beside the gallery's old furnace, feeling the residual warmth from a recent fire, and noticed a corner of charred paper wedged behind the grate that had escaped complete incineration. He carefully extracted the fragment with tweezers, revealing faint printed text that appeared to be some kind of shipping document. Martinez leaned in, squinting at the partially legible header that mentioned art supplies, though the significance of the Belgian address stamp wasn't immediately clear to either detective.
E12.narrative:
Inspector Rothwell carefully smoothed the charred fragment onto a clean evidence sheet, his brow furrowing as Martinez positioned a lamp overhead. The partial manifest listed quantities of pigments, canvas, and frame materials—all seemingly innocuous gallery supplies. But as Rothwell's finger traced the singed edge, he noticed handwritten notations in the margin: "G.H. - usual arrangement - 15%" followed by what appeared to be a date from three weeks prior. The Belgian supplier's name, though partially destroyed by flame, matched one he'd seen earlier while reviewing Edmund's private correspondence—letters the victim had kept locked in his desk drawer, letters that had mentioned "concerns about Geoffrey's judgment" and "protecting the family legacy."

"G.H.," Martinez murmured, pulling out her notebook. "Geoffrey Hartley was managing the gallery's procurement, wasn't he?" She flipped back several pages. "The financial officer mentioned Geoffrey had sole authority over international acquisitions. And that Belgian address—didn't the art fraud division issue a bulletin last year about forgery operations running through Brussels?"

Rothwell stood slowly, the evidence bag dangling from his fingers like an accusation. Someone had tried very hard to destroy this document, feeding it to the furnace shortly after Edmund's collapse. The timing suggested panic, a hasty attempt to eliminate proof of something shameful. If Geoffrey Hartley had been importing fraudulent pieces and taking a percentage, and if Edmund had discovered the scheme on the night of the gala, it provided a compelling motive for murder. Yet something nagged at Rothwell—the deliberateness of the poison, the calculated nature of the crime, seemed at odds with the panicked burning of evidence. "Bag everything from this furnace," he instructed Martinez. "And find out exactly when Geoffrey Hartley was last seen near this room."

E13.description: Inspector Rothwell contacts Interpol's Art Crime division to investigate the Belgian supplier listed on the manifest, requesting any records of fraudulent operations or connections to art forgery networks. Meanwhile, Detective Martinez cross-references the specific art supplies on the manifest with Edmund Hartley's gallery inventory records to identify any discrepancies or unusual orders placed before his death.
E13.narrative:
Inspector Rothwell leaned back in his chair as the Interpol liaison's voice crackled through the speakerphone. "The Belgian supplier—Rousseau et Fils—ceased operations eighteen months ago," the agent reported. "But here's what's interesting: they were investigated for shipping legitimate art supplies with inflated invoices. The extra money was allegedly used to purchase forgeries that would replace originals in private collections." Rothwell's pulse quickened. "Were any British galleries implicated?" A pause, then: "Nothing proven, but Geoffrey Hartley's name appeared in our preliminary interviews. He'd made several trips to Brussels around that time, claimed he was sourcing materials for his brother's gallery." Rothwell exchanged a meaningful glance with Martinez, who was already pulling up Geoffrey's travel records on her laptop.

Martinez's fingers flew across the keyboard as she cross-referenced the manifest against Edmund's official inventory. "James, look at this," she said, highlighting several entries on her screen. "The manifest lists twenty liters of refined linseed oil, specialty pigments, and imported canvas—supplies that never appear in Edmund's purchasing ledgers. But Geoffrey's personal studio account shows a large cash withdrawal three weeks before Edmund's death, the exact amount matching these missing supplies." She clicked to another window. "And here's the troubling part: Geoffrey accessed the gallery's furnace room twice in the week following the murder, according to the security logs. He told the night guard he was disposing of damaged canvases." Rothwell studied the partially burned manifest in the evidence bag before him, its charred edges revealing just enough to condemn—or was it to frame? The hollow ring weighed heavily in his mind; it seemed too delicate, too feminine for Geoffrey's broad hands, yet the financial trail and the attempted destruction of evidence pointed directly at Edmund's own brother.

E14.description: The Scotland Yard forensic laboratory calls Inspector Rothwell with urgent preliminary findings: the champagne glass fragments contain traces of scopolamine, a powerful sedative that would have rendered Edmund Hartley disoriented and compliant before his death. Rothwell immediately drives to the Home Office morgue where the independent pathologist has just begun the full autopsy, hoping to confirm whether the drug was administered before or during the fatal encounter, and whether it matches any substances found in Hartley's system.
E14.narrative:
Inspector Rothwell strode through the sterile corridors of the Home Office morgue, the forensic report burning in his coat pocket like a live coal. The scopolamine discovery had opened a new dimension to Edmund Hartley's murder—premeditation of the coldest variety. As he pushed through the autopsy suite's double doors, he found not only the pathologist but Dr. Marcus Pemberton standing beside the examination table, his distinguished frame draped in a visitor's gown, his expression grave with professional concern.

"Inspector," Pemberton began before Rothwell could speak, his voice carrying that particular authority reserved for Harley Street physicians. "I took the liberty of reviewing the preliminary toxicology at the coroner's request—Edmund was my patient for fifteen years, after all. This scopolamine finding troubles me deeply, but I feel obligated to share something." He paused, producing a leather-bound appointment diary. "Three weeks ago, Vivienne Ashford came to my consulting rooms complaining of severe migraines. She mentioned, quite casually, that Edmund had been behaving erratically, paranoid even. She wondered if stress might be affecting his judgment about the gallery's finances. At the time, I thought she was simply being a concerned business partner." Pemberton's fingers traced the diary entry with practiced precision. "But now I wonder—was she establishing a narrative? Planting seeds that Edmund was mentally unstable, perhaps to explain away any concerning behavior witnesses might report?"

Rothwell felt the familiar electric charge of a breakthrough, his mind already racing toward Vivienne Ashford's calculated misdirection, when Pemberton added quietly, "Of course, she also mentioned that Geoffrey had been pressuring Edmund terribly about selling the gallery to cover gambling debts. She showed me threatening letters Geoffrey had sent—I saw them myself, Inspector

E15.description: Inspector Rothwell arranges separate interrogation rooms at Scotland Yard, bringing in Dr. Pemberton first and laying out the scopolamine evidence, the Belgian forgery connection, and witness statements linking him to the gallery that night. He offers Pemberton a deal: testify against whoever orchestrated Edmund Hartley's murder in exchange for reduced charges of accessory rather than conspiracy to commit murder. Martinez simultaneously places Thomas Blackwood in the adjacent room where he can see Pemberton through the one-way glass, letting him sweat while waiting his turn.
E15.narrative:
Inspector Rothwell leaned back in his chair, studying Dr. Pemberton's composed features across the scarred table. The physician's hands remained steady despite the photographs of the Belgian shipping manifests spread before him, despite the toxicology reports highlighting scopolamine traces. "Your pharmaceutical expertise makes you invaluable to this investigation, Doctor," Rothwell said carefully. "We know someone with medical knowledge had to coordinate Edmund Hartley's poisoning. The arsenic trioxide, the precise dosage—this wasn't amateur work." He paused, letting the weight settle. "Here's what I'm offering: full testimony about who planned this murder, and you walk away with accessory charges. Otherwise, you're looking at conspiracy to commit murder."

Dr. Pemberton's lips curved into something resembling sympathy. "Inspector, I understand your frustration. Edmund's death has shaken us all." He straightened his tie with deliberate calm. "But if you're looking for someone with both pharmaceutical knowledge and motive, you're asking the wrong questions. Yes, I consulted on the gallery's insurance valuations—perfectly legal work. And yes, I was present that evening." He leaned forward, his voice dropping to a confidential register. "But have you truly examined Geoffrey Hartley's background? The man spent two years working for a chemical supply company before joining his father's firm. He had access to the exact compounds you're investigating. And the financial motive?" Pemberton shook his head gravely. "Edmund was planning to cut Geoffrey out of the inheritance entirely. I overheard them arguing about it the week before. Geoffrey was desperate—said something about 'making things right before it's too late.' At the time, I thought he meant reconciliation."

Rothwell felt the familiar electric charge of a breakthrough, but something nagged at him. "And

E16.description: Inspector Rothwell rifled through the stack of correspondence on Edmund Hartley's gallery desk, most of it routine invoices and exhibition inquiries. His fingers paused on a telegram dated two days before the murder, the words "proof you requested regarding the Rembrandt affair" catching his eye, though he had no context yet for what this Rembrandt matter might be or why it would merit such cryptic communication.
E16.narrative:
Inspector Rothwell's attention shifted from the telegram to a leather-bound ledger partially concealed beneath a stack of auction catalogs. He pulled it free, noting the initials "E.H." embossed in gold on the cover. The pages revealed meticulous records of artwork acquisitions, but as he thumbed through the entries, Detective Martinez leaned over his shoulder and tapped a series of notations marked in red ink—each one corresponding to pieces allegedly purchased through a Brussels intermediary.

"Look at this," Martinez said, her voice low. "Every red entry has a secondary notation in the margin—initials 'G.H.' with monetary amounts that don't match the official purchase prices." She traced her finger down the column where Geoffrey Hartley's initials appeared again and again, always alongside inflated figures that suggested substantial overcharges. The most recent entry, dated just three days before Edmund's death, showed a disputed Rembrandt sketch with a notation in Edmund's agitated handwriting: "G.H.—confront immediately regarding Belgian discrepancies. Cannot continue."

Rothwell straightened, his jaw tightening as he considered the implications. "Geoffrey had access to the gallery's purchasing accounts. If Edmund discovered his nephew was skimming funds through fraudulent invoicing..." He didn't need to finish the thought. Martinez was already photographing the pages, her camera flash illuminating what appeared to be a clear financial motive—one that would give Geoffrey Hartley every reason to silence his uncle before the fraud could be exposed. The telegram about "proof" suddenly took on an ominous significance, though neither detective yet understood how deeply the deception ran or who else might be implicated in the scheme.

E17.description: Inspector Rothwell contacts the telegram office to trace the sender's identity and location, requesting copies of any other communications between that sender and Edmund Hartley. He also arranges a meeting with a Rembrandt authentication expert to understand what "proof" regarding the painting might have been significant enough to warrant such communication just before Edmund's death.
E17.narrative:
Inspector Rothwell spread the telegram copies across his desk at the precinct, noting the sender's information the clerk had provided: dispatched from Brussels, paid for in cash, sender listed only as "V. Durant." The authentication expert, Dr. Helena Frost from the Metropolitan Museum, arrived within the hour, her sharp eyes immediately drawn to the photographs of the contested Rembrandt that Edmund had been preparing to unveil. "If someone possessed proof this painting was fraudulent," she explained, adjusting her spectacles, "the financial implications would be catastrophic. Edmund's entire reputation rested on this acquisition. But more intriguing—" she tapped the telegram with a gloved finger, "—is that the sender claims to have proof of *authenticity*, not forgery. That suggests someone wanted Edmund to proceed with the exhibition despite concerns he may have harbored."

"Could Geoffrey Hartley have sent this?" Martinez asked, pulling out the younger Hartley's travel documents. "He was in Belgium three weeks ago, ostensibly purchasing estate furnishings for the gallery renovation. The dates align perfectly with when this telegram was composed."

Rothwell felt the pieces shifting into a darker configuration. He retrieved the ladies' ornate ring from the evidence locker, turning it slowly under his desk lamp. The hollow compartment in the band caught the light—small enough to be overlooked, sophisticated enough to be deliberate. "Dr. Frost, in your experience with European art dealers, would a Belgian supplier of painting materials have any reason to authenticate a Rembrandt?" The expert's brow furrowed. "Absolutely not. Unless..." she paused, choosing her words carefully, "unless the 'supplier' was actually a forger who needed to ensure his work would pass inspection. Geoffrey Hartley would have had both motive and opportunity to orchestrate his uncle's death—inher

E18.description: Inspector Rothwell coordinates with Martinez and four uniformed officers to execute synchronized arrests at precisely 6 AM, hitting all four locations simultaneously to prevent any warning between conspirators. As each suspect is taken into custody, Rothwell personally serves Vivienne Ashford with the warrant while presenting the complete evidence chain: the scopolamine procurement records, Dr. Pemberton's confession implicating her as orchestrator, the forged Rembrandt authentication she commissioned to ruin Edmund, and the telegram proving Edmund had discovered her scheme two days before his murder.
E18.narrative:
The dawn raid had been meticulously planned, but as Inspector Rothwell approached the townhouse of Charlotte Devereaux at half-past five, he found her already awake, dressed immaculately in dove-grey silk, calmly arranging white roses in a crystal vase. She turned with practiced surprise as Martinez announced their presence, her hand fluttering to her throat in a gesture of delicate concern.

"Inspector, how fortuitous—I was just about to telephone the station," she said, her voice trembling with what seemed like barely contained urgency. "I've been awake all night, tormented by conscience. That ring you found, the one with the hollow compartment... I saw Geoffrey handling it the evening before the gallery opening. I thought nothing of it at the time, assumed it was a gift for his wife perhaps, but now..." She moved to her writing desk, retrieving a handkerchief-wrapped bundle with theatrical reluctance. "I found this hidden in the gallery's storage room yesterday—a jeweler's receipt in Geoffrey's name for modifications to a ladies' ring, dated three weeks before Edmund's death. The specifications mention a 'concealed interior mechanism.'" Her eyes glistened as she pressed the paper into Rothwell's hands. "I know Vivienne has been helping with the investigation, sharing gallery records and such, but Inspector, you must understand—Geoffrey had the most to gain from his brother's death. The inheritance, the gallery directorship, everything. Vivienne was merely Edmund's business partner; she had nothing to gain and everything to lose."

Rothwell studied the receipt, his jaw tightening as he noticed the jeweler's stamp and Geoffrey's apparent signature. Martinez caught his eye, nodding toward the door—the other teams would be moving into position at the remaining locations. "Miss Devereaux, you

evidence.E001.description: A ladies' gold ring with an ornate flower design found near the victim's body, appears to have a small hollow compartment in the band

evidence.E002.description: Multiple witnesses report seeing Vivienne and Edmund in an intense private conversation in her office 30 minutes before the opening ceremony

evidence.E003.description: A partially burned shipping manifest found in the gallery's furnace room listing art supplies from a suspicious Belgian supplier

evidence.E004.description: A telegram received at the gallery two days before the event, addressed to Edmund, stating 'I have the proof you requested regarding the Rembrandt affair'

evidence.E005.description: Traces of white powder found on the rim of Edmund's champagne glass shard, initially dismissed as sugar or dust

evidence.E006.description: A waiter recalls Charlotte Devereaux asking him to ensure Edmund received a specific glass from a particular tray, which he thought odd but complied

evidence.E007.description: Gallery security log shows camera 7 in the main hall malfunctioned exactly 10 minutes before the toast and resumed functioning 15 minutes after Edmund's collapse

evidence.E008.description: A small leather notebook found hidden in Edmund's coat pocket containing cryptic notes about paintings, dates, and the initials 'V.A.' circled repeatedly in red ink
